---
title: "Singapore Public Transport Ridership Recovery Across Bus, MRT & LRT"
subtitle: "AAI1001 Team Project Proposal"
author: "LAB-P1 Team 12"
date: "23 June 2026"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: false
    anchor-sections: true
    smooth-scroll: true
    page-layout: full
    theme: cosmo
    css: styles.css
    include-after-body: theme-controls.html
    self-contained: true
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    df-print: paged
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

::: {.report-hero}
::: {.hero-kicker}
AAI1001 Data Engineering and Visualization · Team Project Proposal
:::

# Singapore Public Transport Ridership Recovery Across Bus, MRT & LRT

::: {.hero-summary}
This proposal studies how Singapore's public transport ridership recovered after Covid-19 across bus, MRT and LRT services. The selected Straits Times graphic gives a strong overview of daily rides from 2019 to 2025, but the redesign will make recovery, mode shift and baseline comparison easier to interpret.
:::

::: {.hero-meta}
<span>LAB-P1 Team 12</span>
<span>Thaw Zin · Siyona · Wilson · Troy · Zayar</span>
<span>Source focus: The Straits Times Graphics / Land Transport Authority</span>
:::

::: {.metric-grid}
::: {.metric-card .metric-card-primary}
<span class="metric-label">2025 total</span>
<span class="metric-value">7.540M</span>
<span class="metric-note">average public transport rides per day</span>
:::

::: {.metric-card}
<span class="metric-label">MRT recovery</span>
<span class="metric-value">3.490M</span>
<span class="metric-note">2025 daily rides, above 2019 level</span>
:::

::: {.metric-card}
<span class="metric-label">Bus gap</span>
<span class="metric-value">-258K</span>
<span class="metric-note">2025 bus rides versus 2019 baseline</span>
:::

::: {.metric-card}
<span class="metric-label">LRT scale</span>
<span class="metric-value">209K</span>
<span class="metric-note">2025 daily rides, small but visible in the network mix</span>
:::
:::
:::

# Team Information

::: {.info-panel}
| Item | Details |
|---|---|
| Module | AAI1001 Data Engineering and Visualization |
| Team | LAB-P1 Team 12 |
| Project Title | Singapore Public Transport Ridership Recovery Across Bus, MRT & LRT |
| Original Visualization | Public transport: Average number of rides per day (in '000) |
| Original Source | The Straits Times Graphics / Land Transport Authority |
| Team Members | Thaw Zin, Siyona, Wilson, Troy, Zayar |
:::

# 1. Introduction and Project Overview

## 1.1 Our Project's Aim

::: {.section-lead}
Singapore's public transport ridership has been recovering since the sharp disruption caused by Covid-19, but the recovery has not been equal across transport modes.
:::

Singapore's public transport system is a daily-use infrastructure network, so changes in ridership reveal more than transport volume alone. They also reflect commuter behaviour, hybrid work patterns, rail network expansion, service coverage and first- and last-mile travel demand. In 2025, MRT usage rose above its 2019 level, while bus ridership remained below its pre-pandemic level and LRT ridership slipped slightly from 2024. This creates a useful data story about how commuting patterns have changed across buses, MRT and LRT after the pandemic.

Our project aims to investigate the relationship between **year**, **transport mode**, **average daily ridership** and **post-pandemic recovery** in Singapore's public transport system. Instead of only showing the total number of daily rides, we want to make it easier to compare how each mode contributes to overall ridership and how each mode has recovered relative to 2019.

::: {.question-grid}
::: {.question-card}
<span class="question-index">Q1</span>
How did average daily public transport ridership change from 2019 to 2025?
:::

::: {.question-card}
<span class="question-index">Q2</span>
Which mode, bus, MRT or LRT, contributed most to the post-pandemic recovery?
:::

::: {.question-card}
<span class="question-index">Q3</span>
Has Singapore's public transport ridership fully returned to its pre-Covid-19 pattern, or has the mode mix changed?
:::
:::

::: {.insight-callout}
**Design aim.** The final improved visualization should make the uneven recovery immediately visible: MRT has grown past its pre-pandemic level, buses have not fully recovered, and LRT remains a small but locally important part of the network.
:::

## 1.2 Source & Context

::: {.source-card}
<span class="source-label">Original article</span>
**"More rides on MRT in 2025 as LRT use fell; bus ride numbers still below pre-Covid-19 levels"**  
Published by *The Straits Times* on 30 January 2026, using Land Transport Authority ridership figures.
:::

The chart we selected focuses on public transport. It shows the average number of daily rides, in thousands, for bus, MRT and LRT from 2019 to 2025. According to the article and chart, MRT ridership climbed to **3.49 million daily trips** in 2025, bus ridership averaged **3.841 million daily trips**, and LRT ridership fell slightly to **209,000 daily trips**.

The topic is suitable for this project because it is quantitative, current and socially relevant. Public transport ridership reflects changes in commuting behaviour, remote work, rail network expansion, first- and last-mile travel, and post-pandemic mobility patterns. The original chart already communicates the broad trend, but it creates opportunities for improvement because mode-level changes are not equally easy to compare.

::: {.data-source-grid}
::: {.data-source-card}
<span class="card-kicker">Primary data direction</span>
**LTA public transport ridership**  
Average daily rides by year and transport mode.
:::

::: {.data-source-card}
<span class="card-kicker">Optional enrichment</span>
**Transport network context**  
Station, line or network milestone data where useful for explaining shifts.
:::

::: {.data-source-card}
<span class="card-kicker">Analytical focus</span>
**Recovery and mode mix**  
Mode share, year-on-year change and recovery index against 2019.
:::
:::

These sources allow the project to move beyond a simple stacked total and examine recovery by mode, mode share and year-on-year change.

## 1.2 Our Chosen Visualization

The chosen original visualization is a horizontal stacked bar chart titled **"Public transport"**, with the subtitle **"Average number of rides per day (in '000)"**. It was produced by Straits Times Graphics using Land Transport Authority data.

### 1.2.1 Visualization Structure and Design

::: {.figure-shell}
::: {.figure-header}
<span class="figure-title">Original chart structure</span>
<span class="figure-subtitle">Horizontal stacked bars, one row per year, showing bus, MRT and LRT ridership.</span>
:::

| Encoding | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Row position | Year from 2019 to 2025 |
| Total label | Combined average daily public transport rides, in thousands |
| Bar length | Total daily ridership |
| Bar segments | Bus, MRT and LRT mode contribution |
| Colour | Transport mode category |

::: {.figure-note}
The original chart is useful as a compact overview, but the reader still needs to calculate mode-level recovery and baseline differences manually.
:::
:::

The original visualization is structured as a set of horizontal stacked bars, one row per year:

- Each row represents one year from 2019 to 2025.
- The bold number beside each year shows total average daily ridership across bus, MRT and LRT, in thousands.
- Each stacked bar is split into three coloured segments: bus, MRT and LRT.
- The bus and MRT segments include direct value labels. The LRT segment is visible as a small grey segment at the end of each bar.

The visualization combines several dimensions:

::: {.comparison-table}
| Dimension | How It Appears in the Original Chart |
|---|---|
| Year | One horizontal row for each year from 2019 to 2025 |
| Transport mode | Colour-coded stacked segments for Bus, MRT and LRT |
| Average daily ridership | Segment lengths and selected direct labels |
| Total ridership | Bold number beside each year |
| Post-pandemic recovery | 2019 baseline compared with 2020-2025 values |
:::

This makes the chart useful for showing a broad recovery pattern, but some comparisons are still difficult. Readers can see that total ridership fell sharply in 2020 and then recovered, but they need to calculate differences manually to understand exactly which mode is driving the recovery.

### 1.2.2 The Story It Tells

::: {.story-panel}
::: {.story-stat}
<span class="story-value">7.691M → 5.040M → 7.540M</span>
<span class="story-label">Total daily public transport rides fell sharply in 2020, then recovered close to the 2019 baseline by 2025.</span>
:::
:::

The original visualization tells a story about Singapore's public transport recovery after Covid-19. Total average daily public transport ridership fell from **7.691 million rides in 2019** to **5.040 million rides in 2020**, before recovering steadily to **7.540 million rides in 2025**.

The mode-level story is more nuanced. MRT ridership increased from **3.384 million daily rides in 2019** to **3.490 million in 2025**, meaning it has recovered beyond the pre-pandemic level. Bus ridership increased slightly from 2024 to 2025, but its 2025 figure of **3.841 million** remains below the 2019 figure of **4.099 million**. LRT ridership is much smaller than the other two modes and slipped slightly from **210,000 daily rides in 2024** to **209,000 in 2025**.

::: {.analysis-grid}
::: {.analysis-card}
<span class="card-kicker">Total recovery</span>
The system has nearly returned to its pre-pandemic daily volume, but the recovery is not evenly distributed across modes.
:::

::: {.analysis-card}
<span class="card-kicker">MRT shift</span>
MRT appears to be gaining relative importance because its 2025 ridership exceeds the 2019 level.
:::

::: {.analysis-card}
<span class="card-kicker">Bus gap</span>
Buses remain the largest single mode, but 2025 bus rides are still lower than the 2019 baseline.
:::
:::

Overall, the original chart has a meaningful story, but the current design makes the reader work too hard to extract the most important analytical comparisons. Our project will preserve the useful stacked yearly overview while improving readability, highlighting recovery relative to 2019, and making mode-level changes easier to compare.

# 2. Critical Analysis of Original Visualization

::: {.section-lead}
What this section does: We evaluate the original chart's design choices by identifying what it does well and what limits its analytical value. These points directly motivate the redesign decisions in Section 3.
:::

### Strengths

What works well

::: {.critique-grid}
::: {.critique-card .positive}
#### Stacked yearly bars combine total and mode context

The chart's main strength is that it combines total ridership and mode composition in a single view. The bold total beside each year tells readers the overall number of average daily rides, while the stacked bar shows how that total is split across bus, MRT and LRT. This gives useful context because the same total can have different mode mixes.
:::

::: {.critique-card .positive}
#### Consistent colour encoding across transport modes

The same transport modes are represented with consistent colours throughout the chart. Bus is shown in dark navy, MRT in blue and LRT in grey. This makes it easy for readers to track each mode across the different years.
:::

::: {.critique-card .positive}
#### Multi-variable analysis in a single chart

The chart successfully combines year, transport mode, mode-level ridership and total ridership in one figure. This is useful because the article's story depends on comparing multiple modes across time, rather than looking at only one transport type.
:::

::: {.critique-card .positive}
#### Clear legend and descriptive subtitle

The chart title, subtitle, legend and source note are clear. The subtitle states the measurement unit, "Average number of rides per day (in '000)", and the source note credits the Land Transport Authority. These elements help readers understand what is being measured before interpreting the values.
:::
:::

### Weaknesses

What limits analysis

::: {.critique-grid}
::: {.critique-card .risk}
#### Mode-level comparison requires extra visual effort

The chart places year labels, total labels and stacked bars on the same row, but the visual emphasis is uneven. The total numbers are bold and easy to see, while some segment labels sit inside the bars and compete with the bar colours. Readers can read the chart, but it takes extra effort to compare mode-level changes across years.
:::

::: {.critique-card .risk}
#### Pre-pandemic baseline is not visually emphasised

The chart shows one stacked bar per year, but it does not provide a clear visual reference for the 2019 pre-pandemic baseline. Since the article's headline emphasizes whether ridership has returned to pre-Covid-19 levels, readers must mentally compare every later year against 2019.
:::

::: {.critique-card .risk}
#### Stacked bars make MRT and LRT comparison less direct

In a stacked bar, only the first segment has a common baseline. Bus is easy to compare because it starts from the same left edge each year, but MRT starts after the bus segment, making year-to-year MRT comparison less direct. LRT is even harder to compare because it is a very small end segment.
:::

::: {.critique-card .risk}
#### Small LRT segment is visually compressed

The LRT segment is much smaller than the bus and MRT segments, so it becomes visually compressed. Even though the article notes that LRT ridership fell slightly in 2025, the decline from 210,000 to 209,000 daily rides is almost impossible to detect from the bar length alone.
:::

::: {.critique-card .risk}
#### No direct value annotations

The bus and MRT segments have direct value labels, but the small LRT segment is not directly labelled in the visible chart. Readers need to subtract bus and MRT values from the total to estimate LRT ridership. The chart also does not directly label year-on-year changes or recovery relative to 2019.
:::

::: {.critique-card .risk}
#### Colour palette has accessibility concerns

The chart uses a restrained colour palette, but some older-year segments are faded while the 2025 row is strongly highlighted. This helps draw attention to the latest year, but it also makes precise comparison across years harder. The LRT grey segment is especially subtle because it is small and low contrast.
:::
:::

::: {.recommendation-callout}
**Redesign direction.** The improved visualization should keep the compact yearly overview, but add explicit baseline markers, mode-level comparison support, labelled LRT values and change metrics that explain what has recovered, what has not, and how the mode mix changed.
:::

# 3. Proposed Improvements

::: {.placeholder-panel}
This section is intentionally left as the next working section. It should convert the weaknesses in Section 2 into specific, justified redesign decisions.
:::

| # | Weakness Addressed | Improvement | Method / Technique |
|---|---|---|---|
| I1 |  |  |  |
| I2 |  |  |  |
| I3 |  |  |  |
| I4 |  |  |  |
| I5 |  |  |  |
| I6 |  |  |  |

# 4. Data Sources & Data Quality

## 4.1 Selected Datasets

::: {.placeholder-panel}
To be completed after confirming the exact LTA dataset source and any supporting context data.
:::

Primary Dataset

| Field | Details |
|---|---|
| Dataset name |  |
| Source |  |
| Variables needed |  |
| Role in project |  |

Secondary Dataset

| Field | Details |
|---|---|
| Dataset name |  |
| Source |  |
| Variables needed |  |
| Role in project |  |

## 4.2 Justification for Dataset Selection

::: {.placeholder-panel}
To be completed.
:::

## 4.3 Raw Data Structure and Known Challenges

::: {.placeholder-panel}
To be completed.
:::

### 4.3.1 Public Transport Ridership Raw Layout

::: {.placeholder-panel}
To be completed.
:::

### 4.3.2 Supporting Context Data Raw Layout

::: {.placeholder-panel}
To be completed.
:::

### 4.3.3 Identified Data Quality Challenges

| Issue | Planned Resolution |
|---|---|
|  |  |
|  |  |

# 5. Data Engineering Workflow

## 5.1 Planned Data Engineering Pipeline

::: {.placeholder-panel}
To be completed.
:::

Pipeline Principle

::: {.placeholder-panel}
Each stage should produce a named intermediate object so the workflow is reproducible, inspectable and easy to rerun.
:::

| Stage | Input | Main Operations | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Ingest Raw Ridership Data |  |  |  |
| 2. Standardise Units, Years and Mode Labels |  |  |  |
| 3. Reshape to Long (Tidy) Format |  |  |  |
| 4. Derive Mode Shares, Recovery Index and Change Metrics |  |  |  |
| 5. Join, Validate and Export Visualization-Ready Objects |  |  |  |

## 5.2 Planned Cleaning and Transformation Decisions

::: {.placeholder-panel}
To be completed.
:::

| # | Issue | Decision | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| D1 |  |  |  |
| D2 |  |  |  |
| D3 |  |  |  |
| D4 |  |  |  |
| D5 |  |  |  |

## 5.3 Target Output Schema

::: {.placeholder-panel}
To be completed.
:::

ridership_data · Primary Visualization Table

| Column | R Type | Source | Unit / Range | Role in Chart |
|---|---|---|---|---|
|  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |

mode_labels · Annotation Table

| Column | R Type | Source | Unit / Range | Role in Chart |
|---|---|---|---|---|
|  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |

## 5.4 Illustrative Code Structure

::: {.placeholder-panel}
To be completed.
:::

## 5.5 Reproducibility Considerations

::: {.placeholder-panel}
To be completed.
:::

# 6. Data Analysis Plan

## 6.1 What This Analysis Is For

::: {.placeholder-panel}
To be completed.
:::

## 6.2 Measures We Will Compute

::: {.placeholder-panel}
To be completed.
:::

| Measure | Formula / Method | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
|  |  |  |
|  |  |  |

## 6.3 Planned Analyses

::: {.placeholder-panel}
To be completed.
:::

### A1 — Recovery Gap from 2019 by Mode

::: {.placeholder-panel}
To be completed.
:::

### A2 — Mode Share Shift Across Bus, MRT and LRT

::: {.placeholder-panel}
To be completed.
:::

### A3 — Year-on-Year Ridership Recovery Momentum

::: {.placeholder-panel}
To be completed.
:::

## 6.4 Summary: What We Produce (2025)

| ID | Question Answered (2025) | Output | Supports Which Insight | Data Needed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A1 |  |  |  |  |
| A2 |  |  |  |  |
| A3 |  |  |  |  |

# 7. Planned Work Distribution

::: {.placeholder-panel}
To be completed with final task ownership.
:::

| Team Member | Primary Tasks | Est. Hours |
|---|---|---|
| Thaw Zin |  |  |
| Siyona |  |  |
| Wilson |  |  |
| Troy |  |  |
| Zayar |  |  |
| All Members |  |  |

# 8. References

## 8.1 Article Source

::: {.placeholder-panel}
The Straits Times article URL and citation details will be finalized here.
:::

## 8.2 Data Sources

::: {.placeholder-panel}
LTA and supporting data source citations will be finalized here.
:::

## 8.3 Design Reference

::: {.placeholder-panel}
Design and accessibility references will be finalized here if used.
:::

# 9. Appendix: Data Dictionary

::: {.placeholder-panel}
To be completed after the target output schema is confirmed.
:::

## A.1 ridership_data — Primary Visualization Table

| Column | R Type | Source | Unit / Range | Role in Chart |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| year |  |  |  |  |
| mode |  |  |  |  |
| daily_rides_k |  |  |  |  |
| recovery_index |  |  |  |  |

## A.2 mode_labels — Annotation Table

| Column | R Type | Source | Unit / Range | Role in Chart |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| year |  |  |  |  |
| mode |  |  |  |  |
| label_value |  |  |  |  |
| label_position |  |  |  |  |
